title: Jamie Jones-Buchanan

[[Austin Buchanan]] (half-brother) }} }} '''Jamie Daniel Peter Jones-Buchanan''' {{postnominals|country=GBR|size=100%|MBE}} (born 1 August 1981) is a British [[rugby league]] coach, former professional player, and the Chief Executive of the [[Leeds Rhinos]].{{Cite web |last=Sky Sports |date=17 March 2025 |title=Leeds Rhinos: Sky Sports' Jamie Jones-Buchanan to become CEO as Gary Hetherington departure confirmed |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12204/13327893/leeds-rhinos-sky-sports-jamie-jones-buchanan-to-become-ceo-as-gary-hetherington-departure-confirmed |url-status=live}} He was briefly the interim head coach for Leeds in the [[Super League XXVII]] season. A {{rlp|sr}} forward, he played for his entire professional career with Leeds, winning seven Super League Grand Finals, three World Club Challenges, three League Leaders' Shields and one Challenge Cup Final with the club. He played for [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] at international level. ==Background== Jones-Buchanan was born in [[Leeds]], [[West Yorkshire]], England. He is the half-brother of professional [[rugby league]] footballers [[Jodie Broughton]], [[Austin Buchanan]] and Brooke Broughton. He began playing rugby at [[Stanningley SARLFC]] and progressed through the Leeds Academy signing for the club when he was 15 years old. ==Playing career== His Leeds début was on 7 May 1999 against [[Wakefield Trinity]]. Jones-Buchanan played for [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] from the interchange bench in their [[2004 Super League Grand Final]] victory against [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford]].{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/3746300.stm|title=2004 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=2004-10-16|access-date=2004-10-16}} As [[Super League IX]] champions, the Leeds club faced [[2004 NRL season]] premiers, [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs|Canterbury]] in the [[2005 World Club Challenge]]. Jones-Buchanan played at {{rlp|sr}} and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in Leeds' 39–32 victory.{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/4209965.stm|title=2005 World Club Challenge|publisher=BBC|date=2005-02-04|access-date=2005-02-04}} Jones-Buchanan played for Leeds in the [[2005 Challenge Cup]] Final from the interchange bench in their loss against [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]].{{cite news|title=Hull stun Leeds in Challenge Cup|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/4169230.stm|access-date=17 August 2013|newspaper=[[BBC News]]|date=27 August 2005}} He played for the Leeds club from the interchange bench in their [[2005 Super League Grand Final]] loss against Bradford. Jones-Buchanan started in the 2007 [[Super League XIV#Grand Final|Super League XIV Grand Final]] victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], scoring the final try in a historic 33–6 win at [[Old Trafford]].{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7041479.stm|title=2007 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=2007-10-13|access-date=2007-10-13}} In February 2008, Jones-Buchanan helped the Leeds side secure their second world club title as they defeated [[2007 NRL season]] premiers, [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]] in the [[2008 World Club Challenge]] at [[Elland Road]].{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7263513.stm|title=2008 World Club Challenge|publisher=BBC|date=2008-02-29|access-date=2008-02-29}} Jones-Buchanan played in 2008's [[Super League XIII#Grand Final|Super League XIII Grand Final]] victory over St. Helens.{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7642170.stm|title=2008 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=2008-10-04|access-date=2008-10-05}} He was named in the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#England|England]] squad for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]].{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7656553.stm|title=Purdham earns World Cup call-up|publisher=BBC|date=2008-10-07|access-date=2008-10-07}} Jones-Buchanan's [[testimonial match]] at Leeds took place in 2009. That year Jones-Buchanan also played a key role in Leeds winning the [[League Leaders' Shield]]{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/8246177.stm|title=2009 League Leaders Shield|publisher=BBC|date=2009-09-11|access-date=2009-09-11}} This was followed by the Leeds club becoming the first team in the Super League era to win three titles in a row as they defeated St Helens once again at Old Trafford winning 18–10.{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8290503.stm|title=2009 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=2009-10-10|access-date=2009-10-10}} [[File:Jamie Jones-Buchanan England.jpg|thumb|left|Jones-Buchanan representing England]] [[File:Jamie Jones-Buchanan.jpg|thumb|right|Jones-Buchanan playing for the Leeds Rhinos]] Jones-Buchanan did not enjoy the same level of success in the [[Challenge Cup]] however. Having lost to [[Hull F.C.]] in 2005, Jones-Buchanan started all three of Leeds successive losses at [[Wembley Stadium]] between 2010 and 2012: the [[2010 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]],{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/8938067.stm|title=Leeds 6–30 Warrington|publisher=BBC|date=28 August 2010|access-date=22 November 2019|first=Ged|last=Scott}}{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |location=London |work=The Guardian |title=Chris Hicks hat-trick leads Warrington to Challenge Cup triumph over Leeds |first=Andy |last=Wilson |date=28 August 2010 |access-date=22 November 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100831105934/http://www.guardian.co.uk/sport/2010/aug/28/leeds-rhinos-warrington-wolves-challenge-cup-final |archive-date=31 August 2010 |url-status=live |df=dmy }} the [[2011 Challenge Cup]] Final which was lost to [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]],{{cite news|last=Scott|first=Ged|title=Wigan's Challenge Cup win a team effort - Joel Tomkins|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/14695883|access-date=21 November 2019|newspaper=[[BBC Sport]]|date=27 August 2011}} and the [[2012 Challenge Cup]] loss to Warrington.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2012/aug/25/leeds-warrington-challenge-cup |title=Warrington's battered Brett Hodgson recovers to see off Leeds in final|date=25 August 2012|work=Guardian UK|access-date=20 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2012/oct/06/warrington-leeds-grand-final|title=Leeds' Kevin Sinfield stars in Grand Final triumph against Warrington|publisher=Guardian|date=6 October 2012|access-date=20 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19362692|title=Leeds Rhinos 18–35 Warrington Wolves|date=25 August 2012|work=BBC Sport|access-date=20 November 2019}}{{cite web |url=http://www.warringtonwolves.org/warrington-wolves-are-challenge-cup-winners-2012-5628 |title=Warrington Wolves Are Challenge Cup Winners 2012! |date=25 August 2012 |work=Warrington Wolves Official Site |access-date=20 November 2019 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131217230118/http://www.warringtonwolves.org/warrington-wolves-are-challenge-cup-winners-2012-5628 |archive-date=17 December 2013 |df=dmy}} However, along with teammates [[Kevin Sinfield]], [[Danny McGuire]], [[Rob Burrow]] and [[Ryan Bailey (rugby league)|Ryan Bailey]] he continued his period of unprecedented success in the Grand Final. All five players, who had emerged from the club's academy would go on to win six Grand Finals together defeating old foes St. Helens in [[2011 Super League Grand Final|2011]]{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/oct/08/super-league-grand-final-match-report?newsfeed=true|title=Leeds claim Grand Final glory as inspired Rob Burrow sinks St Helens|date=8 October 2011|work=Guardian|access-date=21 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/8815944/St-Helens-16-Leeds-32-match-report.html|title=St Helens 16 Leeds 32|date=8 October 2011|work=Daily Telegraph|access-date=21 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/9606230.stm|title=2011 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=2011-10-08|access-date=2011-10-08}} before over turning Warrington in the [[2012 Super League Grand Final]] when Leeds made more history by winning the title after finishing fifth two years running.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19805055|title=2012 Grand Final|publisher=BBC|date=2012-10-06|access-date=2012-10-06}}{{cite news|title=Grand Final: Warrington 18-26 Leeds|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/19805055|access-date=20 November 2019|newspaper=BBC Sport|date=6 October 2012}} As well as winning back to back titles, the Leeds side had also started 2012 by becoming World Champions for a third time as they defeated [[Manly Warringah Sea Eagles|Manly]] at a sold out [[Headingley Rugby Stadium|Headingley]] in the [[2012 World Club Challenge]].{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/17031266|title=2012 World Club Challenge|publisher=BBC|date=2012-02-17|access-date=2012-02-17}} In 2013, Jones-Buchanan crossed for a try in the [[2013 World Club Challenge]] against [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]] but the Leeds outfit lost out to the [[2012 NRL season]] premiers 18–14 at Headingley.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/21532355|title=2013 World Club Challenge|publisher=BBC|date=2013-02-22|access-date=2013-02-22}} Jones-Buchanan finally got his hands on the [[2014 Challenge Cup]] when Leeds defeated the [[Castleford Tigers]] 23–10 at Wembley Stadium.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/aug/23/catleford-leeds-challenge-cup-final-match-report|title=Leeds lift Challenge Cup after Ryan Hall's double stuns Castleford|publisher=Guardian|date=23 August 2014|access-date=13 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/28884420|title=2014 Challenge Cup Final|publisher=BBC|date=2014-08-23|access-date=2014-08-23}} He missed the [[2015 Challenge Cup]] Final due to an injury suffered in the semi-final win over St Helens.{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/33748000|title=Jones-Buchanan ruled out for remainder of the season|publisher=BBC|date=2015-08-01|access-date=2015-08-01}} He played in the [[2017 Super League Grand Final]] victory over Castleford at Old Trafford.{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2017/oct/07/castleford-v-leeds-grand-final-2017-live-super-league-rugby?page=with:block-59d9128de4b00593b4d67a05#liveblog-navigation|title=Castleford 6-24 Leeds: Grand Final 2017 – as it happened|publisher=Guardian|date=7 October 2017|access-date=5 November 2019}}{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/41536394|title=Grand Final 2017: Castleford 6-24 Leeds Rhinos|date=7 October 2017|work=BBC Sport|access-date=9 October 2017}}{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/oct/07/castleford-leeds-super-league-grand-final-match-report|title=Danny McGuire guides Leeds to Grand Final success over Castleford|date=7 October 2017|work=Guardian|access-date=9 October 2017}} Jones-Buchanan was appointed [[Member of the Order of the British Empire]] (MBE) in the [[2022 New Year Honours]] for services to rugby league football and the community in Leeds.{{London Gazette|issue=63571|supp=y|page=N17|date=1 January 2022}} ==Career after playing== He took up an assistant coach role of [[Leeds Rhinos]] after retiring as a player, and stepped in as interim head coach following [[Richard Agar]]'s sacking. In March 2025 it was announced that Jones-Buchanan would become the Leeds Chief Executive in late 2025, following the retirement of [[Gary Hetherington]]. ==Personal life== He is married to Emma Jones-Buchanan, whom he met when he was 14, and has four children. He is a devout Christian and attends church on Sundays.http://www.yorkshireeveningpost.co.uk/news/latest-news/leeds_rhinos_simple_life_for_jamie_jones_buchanan_interview_1_2250462 {{Dead link|date=February 2022}} He has also supported the [[White Ribbon Campaign]], a charity that encourages men to challenge violence against women.{{Cite web|url=http://wrcuk.wordpress.com/tag/white-ribbon-day/|title=White ribbon day}} Away from rugby, Jamie is a huge part of his local community in Leeds. He regularly attends Christian worship across the country to speak about his own life journey and is a trustee of the Leeds Rhinos Foundation, the charitable trust who work in the Leeds community.{{cite web |url=http://www.therhinos.co.uk/news/england-star-backs-importance-of-sport-in-schools-6276 |title=England star backs importance of sport in Schools : News : Leeds Rhinos {{!}} Official Site |website=www.therhinos.co.uk |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160814031319/http://www.therhinos.co.uk/news/england-star-backs-importance-of-sport-in-schools-6276 |archive-date=2016-08-14}} He was also a governor at his former school [[Priesthorpe School]] in Leeds but is no more.{{Cite web |url=http://www.leedsrhinosfoundation.org/news/21064.php |title=Jamie Jones Buchanan, Trustee of the Foundation backs importance of sport in Schools : Leeds Rhinos Foundation | Official website |access-date=23 June 2016 |archive-date=10 August 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160810105623/http://www.leedsrhinosfoundation.org/news/21064.php |url-status=dead }} == Media == [[File:Jamie_Jones-Buchanan_on_stage_at_The_Awakening._LEEDS_2023.jpg|thumb|Jamie Jones-Buchanan on stage at The Awakening. [[Leeds 2023|LEEDS 2023]]]] Years ago, Jones-Buchanan began an interest in journalism, and reporting on sport and Rugby League in general. What began as a blog on the Rhinos official website soon became a monthly column in Rugby League magazine [[Rugby League World]]. From here Jones-Buchanan joined forces with childhood friend Alex Simmons on a new TV and radio project called Rugby AM. He joined the Rugby AM team in December 2013 and has been working to grow Rugby AM. He uses his spare time doing some form of work for Rugby AM.{{cite web |url=http://www.rugbyam.co.uk/ |title=Home |website=rugbyam.co.uk}} In 2016, Jones-Buchanan took on another new role, that of acting. Jones-Buchanan made his stage début in the [[Red Ladder Theatre Company]] production [[Leeds Lads]]. Fans had a chance to see Jamie in the play which ran between Friday 17 June 2016 and Saturday 25 June 2016 at the Carriageworks Theatre in Leeds.{{cite web| url = http://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/news/analysis/jamie-jones-buchanan-leeds-rhinos-star-makes-stage-debut-in-wartime-drama-1-7966882| url-status = dead| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160617142445/http://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/news/analysis/jamie-jones-buchanan-leeds-rhinos-star-makes-stage-debut-in-wartime-drama-1-7966882| archive-date = 2016-06-17| title = Jamie Jones-Buchanan: Leeds Rhinos star makes stage debut in wartime drama -
